era_context: Mid-17th century: The Restoration of Charles II in England marks the end of the Interregnum and the return of monarchy; the Qing Dynasty consolidating Manchu rule over China; Louis XIV consolidating absolute power in France; the Scientific Revolution advancing in Europe; European colonial competition intensifying in Asia, Africa, and the Americas; the Ottoman Empire in slow decline.

events.title: Charles II restored to English throne
events.description: King Charles II is restored to the English throne on May 29, 1660, ending the Interregnum (1649–1660) and the Commonwealth government under Oliver Cromwell. Charles returns from exile in France and is welcomed by Parliament and the English people. The Restoration marks the end of the English Civil War and the return of monarchy, the House of Lords, and the established Church of England. The event reshapes English politics, culture, and religion. Charles II will reign for 25 years (1660–1685) and will be known as the 'Merry Monarch' for his patronage of the arts and sciences.
key_figures: Edward Montagu, 1st Earl of Sandwich (facilitating the Restoration)
Richard Cromwell (son of Oliver Cromwell, deposed)

sources.name: English state papers and parliamentary records

events.certainty_note: The Restoration is extensively documented in parliamentary records, state papers, and contemporary diaries.

events.title: Execution of regicides (those who signed Charles I's death warrant)
events.description: Following the Restoration of Charles II, several of the regicides (those who signed the death warrant of Charles I in 1649) are executed. The executions include Oliver Cromwell (exhumed and executed posthumously), Thomas Harrison, Michael Livesey, and others. The executions serve as both revenge and a warning against future regicide. The trials and executions are extensively documented and become a significant part of Restoration politics and culture.

sources.name: English state papers and legal records

events.certainty_note: The executions are documented in state papers, trial records, and contemporary diaries.

events.title: Act of Navigation reinforces English mercantilism
events.description: The English Parliament passes the Act of Navigation (also known as the Navigation Act of 1660), which reinforces mercantilist policies and restricts colonial trade. The act requires that goods imported into England or its colonies must be carried in English ships or ships of the country of origin. The act aims to increase English shipping and commercial power and to integrate colonial economies into the English mercantile system. The Navigation Acts will become a major source of tension between England and its American colonies in the 18th century.
events.certainty_note: The Act of Navigation is documented in parliamentary records and colonial correspondence.

events.title: Royal Society of London founded (informal establishment)
events.description: A group of natural philosophers, including Robert Boyle, Robert Hooke, Christopher Wren, and others, formally establish the Royal Society of London for the Advancement of Natural Philosophy. The Society begins meeting regularly to discuss and conduct experiments in natural philosophy (science). The Society will receive a royal charter from Charles II in 1662, but its founding in 1660 marks the beginning of organized scientific inquiry in England. The Society becomes a major center for the Scientific Revolution and will publish the Philosophical Transactions, the first scientific journal.
events.certainty_note: The founding of the Royal Society is traditionally dated to 1660, though the formal charter was granted in 1662. The exact date of the first meeting is debated by scholars.

events.title: Treaty of Madrid between England and Spain
events.description: England and Spain sign the Treaty of Madrid, which establishes peace and recognizes the Restoration of Charles II. The treaty reflects the shifting balance of power in Europe, with England emerging as a major power and Spain in decline. The treaty also addresses colonial disputes and trade issues between the two nations. The agreement marks a significant moment in English foreign policy and the beginning of England's rise to naval supremacy.

sources.name: English state papers and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Madrid is documented in English and Spanish diplomatic records.
cross_references: 1667-001 (Treaty of Breda, further reshaping European balance)

events.title: Shunzhi Emperor dies; Kangxi Emperor's father passes
events.description: The Shunzhi Emperor, the seventh emperor of the Qing Dynasty, dies in 1661 (some sources date his death to 1660). His death marks the end of his brief reign (1643–1661) and leads to the accession of his son, the Kangxi Emperor, at age seven. The Shunzhi Emperor's reign was marked by the consolidation of Qing power following the Manchu conquest of 1644. His death sets the stage for the Kangxi Emperor's long and successful reign.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing)

events.certainty_note: The Shunzhi Emperor's death is traditionally dated to 1661, but some sources place it in 1660. The exact date is debated by scholars.

events.title: Portuguese Restoration War continues against Spain
events.description: The Portuguese Restoration War (1640–1668), fought between Portugal and Spain over Portuguese independence, continues with Portuguese forces resisting Spanish attempts to reconquer the country. Portugal, under King John IV, maintains its independence despite Spanish military pressure. The war reflects the decline of Spanish power and the rise of Portugal as an independent nation. Portugal will eventually gain formal recognition of its independence in 1668 with the Treaty of Lisbon.

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and military accounts

events.certainty_note: The Portuguese Restoration War is documented in Portuguese and Spanish records.
cross_references: 1668-001 (Treaty of Lisbon, Spanish recognition of Portuguese independence)

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the Shunzhi Emperor's death is sometimes placed in 1661 rather than 1660; sources are not entirely consistent. The founding date of the Royal Society is traditionally 1660, but some scholars argue that the Society was not formally established until 1662 with the royal charter. The claim that the Act of Navigation of 1660 was the first such act is incorrect; the first Navigation Act was passed in 1651. The Treaty of Madrid is sometimes dated to 1667 rather than 1660; sources are inconsistent on the exact date.
historiographic_note: The year 1660 is exceptionally well-documented for English events, with abundant written records from parliamentary, state, and personal sources. Samuel Pepys's diary provides a detailed contemporary account of the Restoration and its immediate aftermath. Chinese imperial events are documented in the Qing Shilu and court records, though Western historiography often emphasizes English events. Portuguese and Spanish events are documented in their respective archives but may be underrepresented in English-language histories. The Restoration is a pivotal moment in English history and has been extensively studied by historians, resulting in rich documentation and analysis. Indigenous perspectives on European colonial activities are largely absent from surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Charles II's Restoration begins his 25-year reign
Execution of regicides leads to Act of Indemnity and Oblivion
Navigation Act of 1660 leads to Plantation Duty Act
Royal Society founding leads to royal charter
Shunzhi Emperor's death leads to Kangxi Emperor's accession
Portuguese Restoration War continues toward Spanish recognition
